behaviors.description: Only files changed in the pull request are analyzed — unchanged files are not scanned.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is at the GitHub API integration. A unit test with hardcoded 'changed files' would pass even if: the actual GitHub API returns files in different format, authentication fails, base branch comparison doesn't work, or pagination breaks with many files. Must verify actual GitHub API integration or realistic HTTP mocks that simulate GitHub's response behavior.
behaviors.test_description: An integration or system test that verifies: (1) the action queries the correct GitHub API endpoint for changed files in the PR; (2) the API response is parsed correctly (handles different response formats); (3) pagination works for PRs with >30 changed files; (4) only files in the API response are scanned, no extraneous files; (5) error handling if the API call fails or times out.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test description requires GitHub API interaction (System level)—can't be verified with unit mocks alone.

behaviors.description: Findings are grouped by severity (error, warning, info) in the posted PR comment.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary spans two system layers: (1) the grouping logic depends on actual linter output format (severity field may vary), and (2) posting to GitHub requires API integration. A unit test with idealized findings would pass even if: the real linter output has missing/misnamed severity fields, the GitHub API rejects the comment payload format, or Markdown formatting breaks in actual GitHub UI. Must test with realistic linter output and verify GitHub API interaction.
behaviors.test_description: An integration or system test that: (1) runs the actual linter on test files to generate realistic findings; (2) verifies findings are grouped correctly by severity (error, warning, info) with consistent ordering; (3) formats the grouped findings into a GitHub comment payload; (4) posts to GitHub (or mocks the GitHub API call at HTTP level); (5) verifies the posted comment renders correctly with findings in correct groups.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires both realistic linter output format and GitHub API interaction—pure unit test with mocked findings would miss real failures.

behaviors.description: A pull request that touches 200 files completes analysis within the 10-minute Actions timeout.
behaviors.justification: The failure is defined in the context of 'Actions timeout.' An Integration test with 200 local files (no GitHub API calls) could catch algorithm complexity issues (e.g., O(n²) loops), but would miss the cumulative latency of GitHub API calls (fetching changed files, posting results) that contribute to timeout in actual Actions runs. System-level testing with realistic API latency is needed to catch the real failure boundary.
behaviors.test_description: A system test that: (1) creates or uses a PR with exactly 200 changed files (mix of file types the linter handles); (2) runs the full action pipeline including GitHub API calls (or mocks with realistic latency); (3) measures total elapsed time from start to completion; (4) asserts elapsed time is under 10 minutes (or the configured Actions timeout); (5) logs detailed timing breakdown (API calls, per-file analysis, grouping, posting) to identify bottlenecks.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test must run full pipeline with 200 files and realistic latency to catch timeout in production Actions environment.

behaviors.description: When the linter process crashes on a malformed file, the remaining files are still analyzed and results posted.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is subprocess crash handling, not just exception logic. A unit test mocking an in-process exception would pass even if: the actual subprocess crashes in ways that signal-exit the entire action, resource cleanup fails, or the process state is left corrupt. Subprocess crash behavior is fundamentally different from in-process exceptions. Must test with actual subprocess (which can fail, not just raise), requiring Integration level (local subprocess testing).
behaviors.test_description: An integration test that: (1) creates a malformed test file that causes the linter subprocess to crash or exit with an error code; (2) creates a set of valid test files to analyze; (3) runs the action pipeline with the malformed file mixed in; (4) verifies the action does not crash (handles subprocess exit gracefully); (5) verifies all valid files were still analyzed and results collected; (6) verifies findings are posted to GitHub (or mock); (7) optionally tests multiple crash scenarios (signal kill, segfault simulation, etc.).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires actual subprocess crash behavior—can't be accurately simulated with mocked in-process exceptions.

behaviors.description: Duplicate findings on the same line from overlapping rules are deduplicated in the output.
behaviors.justification: This is pure grouping and filtering logic with no I/O, external APIs, or subprocess dependencies. The deduplication algorithm can be tested entirely in isolation with sample or mocked findings. The failure modes (wrong dedup key, broken filtering logic) are all caught at the algorithm level and don't depend on actual linter behavior or GitHub integration.
behaviors.test_description: A unit test that: (1) creates a set of findings with intentional duplicates (e.g., two rules both flagging the same line with similar messages); (2) runs the deduplication logic; (3) verifies exact duplicates are removed (same line and rule); (4) verifies near-duplicates are handled correctly (same line, different rules—should be kept unless explicitly merged); (5) verifies unique findings are preserved; (6) tests edge cases (empty list, single finding, all duplicates, etc.).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test is pure logic on sample data—doesn't require I/O, APIs, or external dependencies.
